1: wayyāḇōʾû šĕnê hammalʾāḵîm sĕḏōmāh bāʿereḇ — "The two angels arrived at Sodom in the evening." The narrative now calls the two figures malʾāḵîm (messengers/angels) — a title not used in Genesis 18, where they were ʾănāšîm (men). The shift marks a narrative distinction: at Mamre all three were "men"; once they proceed to Sodom, the two are identified as divine messengers on a specific mission. The third figure, who was YHWH himself, remains with Abraham for the intercession (Genesis 18:22). Wĕlôṭ yōšēḇ bĕšaʿar-sĕḏōm — "Lot was sitting in the gateway of the city." Sitting in the gate (šaʿar) indicates civic status — the gate was where commerce, justice, and civic life took place (cf. Ruth 4:1-2; Proverbs 31:23). Lot has a position in Sodom's civic life, which explains his response to the visitors and the Sodomites' resentment of him.
2: wayyōʾmer hinneh ʾaḏōnay sûrû-nāʾ ʾel-bêt ʿaḇdĕkem — "He said, My lords, please turn aside to your servant's house." Lot addresses the visitors as ʾăḏōnay — but with the plural form ʾăḏōnay, which is different from the singular divine title Abraham used in Genesis 18:3. Lot's hospitality offer parallels Abraham's but is less extravagant — he offers shelter and a night's rest. Wĕrakăṣû raglêkem wĕhiškamtem wăhălaktem lĕdarkĕkem — "wash your feet, and you may rise early and go on your way." The explicit mention of early departure ("rise early and go your way") suggests Lot already senses the danger of overnight visitors in Sodom.
3: wayyiṣṣar bām mĕʾōḏ wayyāsurû ʾēlāyw wayyāḇōʾû ʾel-bêtô — "but he pressed them strongly and they turned aside to him and entered his house." The verb ṣûr (to press, urge strongly) — the Qal imperfect with mĕʾōḏ (very much) indicates vigorous insistence; Lot had to overcome their initial refusal (v2: "No, we will spend the night in the square"). The urgency of Lot's pressing may reflect his knowledge of Sodom's danger to strangers. Wayyaʿaś lāhem mišteh ûmaṣṣôt ʾāpāh wayyōʾkēlû — "He made for them a feast and baked unleavened bread, and they ate." Maṣṣôt (unleavened bread) — the quick bread of urgent hospitality; the same bread will later mark the urgency of the Exodus departure (Exodus 12:15-20,39).
4: ʾanĕšê hāʿîr ʾanĕšê sĕḏōm nāsabbû ʿal-habbayit minnāʿar wĕʿad-zāqēn kol-hāʿām miqqāṣeh — "the men of the city, the men of Sodom, surrounded the house, from young to old, all the people to the last man." The comprehensiveness of the description — "from young to old, all the people to the last man" (miqqāṣeh, from the end/extremity) — establishes that the violence is not a fringe element but the totality of Sodom's male population. The phrase "all the people to the last man" erases any possibility of a hidden righteous remnant inside the city that Abraham's intercession might have located. The verdict of Genesis 18:20-21 ("the outcry is great, their sin very heavy") is embodied: there are not even ten righteous.
5: wĕnēḏĕʿāh ʾōtām — "that we may know them." The verb yāḏaʿ (H3045) in its sexual sense — the same sense as Genesis 4:1 ("Adam knew his wife Eve") and Genesis 4:17 (Cain knew his wife). The demand to "know" the divine visitors sexually is not only a violation of the hospitality code (which protected guests absolutely in ANE culture) but a perversion of the term that is most central to covenant relationship in Genesis: the yāḏaʿ by which God "knows" Abraham (Genesis 18:19) and by which covenant relationship is formed is here deployed as an instrument of violence and domination. The narrative names the sin with the covenant word turned to its opposite purpose.
6: wayyēṣēʾ ʾălêhem lôṭ happetĕḥāh wĕhaddelet sāgar ʾaḥărāyw — "Lot went out to them at the entrance and shut the door behind him." Lot places himself between his guests and the mob — the door shut behind him means he cannot retreat; he stands as the single barrier between the visitors and the crowd. The gesture is one of physical interposition in hospitality-defense, which in the ANE context was a matter of honor: to fail to protect a guest admitted to your house was a profound shame. Lot's inadequate solution (vv7-8) shows his moral confusion, but his instinct to protect the guests at personal risk is honored by the narrative.
7: wayyōʾmar ʾal-nāʾ ʾaḥay tārēʿû — "He said, 'My brothers, please do not do evil.'" The term ʾaḥay ("my brothers") is a social appeal to community solidarity — the same term he uses in the next verse to introduce his offer. The use of rāʿaʿ (to do evil, do harm) in the Hiphil — "do this evil" — names their demand as moral wrong. Lot does not try to argue that the visitors are divine messengers; he appeals on purely ethical grounds that the action is wrong.
8: hinnēh-nāʾ lî šĕtê ḇānôt ʾăšer lōʾ-yāḏĕʿû ʾîš — "Behold, I have two daughters who have not known a man." The verb yāḏaʿ returns immediately — "who have not known a man" is the negative form of the sexual idiom just used in v5. The two daughters who are "not known" are offered in place of the guests the mob wants to "know." Lot's offer is morally indefensible by any standard, including the Torah's later explicit protections of daughters (Leviticus 19:29; Deuteronomy 23:17). The text does not endorse Lot's proposal; it presents the moral bankruptcy of his situation — the hospitality code he is trying to honor has corrupted his moral judgment to the point of sacrificing his children. Peter's description of Lot as "righteous Lot, greatly distressed by the sensual conduct of the wicked" (2 Peter 2:7-8) acknowledges his relative righteousness without endorsing every choice.
9: wayyōʾmĕrû gĕš-hālĕʾāh wayyōʾmĕrû haʾeḥāḏ bāʾ-lāgûr wayyišpōṭ šāpôṭ — "They said, 'Stand back!' And they said, 'This fellow came to sojourn, and he would play the judge!'" The crowd's charge against Lot is revealing: he is a gēr (sojourner, resident alien) who has presumed to judge (šāpaṭ) the native population. The irony is double: (1) Lot's judgment of the mob's action as wrong is entirely correct; (2) God is about to judge Sodom with actual judicial authority (the very mišpāṭ that Abraham appealed to in Genesis 18:25). The mob's rage at Lot for "playing judge" reveals their rejection of any moral accountability — the defining condition that makes judgment inevitable.
10: wayyišlĕḥû hāʾănāšîm ʾet-yādām wayyāḇîʾû ʾet-lôṭ ʾălêhem habbayĕtāh — "but the men reached out their hands and brought Lot into the house to them." The divine visitors act to protect Lot — they extend their hands (šālaḥ yāḏ) to pull him in. The gesture of the outstretched hand reverses: the mob's reaching toward the door (v9: "they pressed hard against Lot and came near to break the door") is countered by the angels' reaching to rescue. The sequence — mob pressing in, angels pulling the endangered person away — is a rescue pattern that recurs in the angel narratives (Daniel 6:22; Acts 12:7-11).
11: wĕʾet-hāʾănāšîm ʾăšer-peteḥ habbayit hikkû bĕsanwērîm miqqāṭōn wĕʿad-gāḏôl wayyilʾû limmĕṣōʾ happātaḥ — "And the men at the entrance of the house they struck with blindness, from small to great, so that they wearied themselves to find the entrance." The word sanwĕrîm (H5575, blindness) — a rare term appearing only here and in 2 Kings 6:18 (where the Aramean army is struck blind before Elisha); it may be a loanword from Akkadian. The deliberate ambiguity — physical blindness or disorientation — does not diminish their persistence: "they wearied themselves to find the entrance." The blindness does not produce repentance; the Sodomites keep groping for the door. Moral blindness and physical blindness compound each other — the judgment on the senses mirrors the judgment on the will.
12: ʾōd-mî lĕḵā pōh ḥātan ûḇāneyḵā ûḇĕnōteyḵā wĕkol ʾăšer-lĕḵā bāʿîr hôṣēʾ min-hammāqôm — "Have you anyone else here? Son-in-law, sons, daughters, or anyone you have in the city — bring them out of the place." The angels' urgency reverses the previous question structure: instead of Lot going out to protect the visitors, the visitors are now focused on getting Lot's household out. The completeness of the summons ("anyone else," all categories) mirrors the completeness of the destruction to come. The māqôm (the place) becomes the technical term for Sodom in its condemned status — the same word used in Abraham's intercession ("spare the whole māqôm," Genesis 18:24,26,28).
13: kî-mašḥîtîm ʾănaḥnû ʾet-hammāqôm hazzeh — "For we are about to destroy this place." The verb šāḥat (Hiphil, to destroy, ruin) — mašḥîtîm (the participle, indicating imminent action) — uses the same root as the "corruption" of Genesis 6:11-12 (tišāḥēt hāʾāreṣ, "the earth was corrupt/being destroyed"). The symmetry is intentional: the pre-flood narrative uses šāḥat for corruption/destruction; the Sodom narrative uses it for divine judgment. Kî-gāḏĕlāh ṣaʿăqātām ʾet-pĕnê YHWH — "for the outcry against them has grown great before YHWH" — the same cry (ṣaʿaqāh) that reached YHWH in 18:20 is here specified as the judicial basis for the destruction.
14: wayyēṣēʾ lôṭ wayyĕdabbēr ʾel-ḥătānāyw lōqĕḥê ḇĕnōtāyw wayyōʾmer qûmû ṣĕʾû min-hammāqôm hazzeh — "Lot went out and spoke to his sons-in-law, those who were pledged to marry his daughters, and said, 'Get up, leave this place.'" Lōqĕḥê ḇĕnōtāyw — "those who were taking his daughters" — the participle lōqĕḥê (ones taking) indicates pledged marriage, not yet consummated (the daughters are described as virgin in v8). Wayhî kimĕṣaḥēq bĕʿênê ḥătānāyw — "He seemed to his sons-in-law to be mocking." Mĕṣaḥēq — the Piel participle of ṣāḥaq — "jesting, laughing at." The same root as Isaac's name and Sarah's laughter. The word that named the miracle-child (the laughter of faith and wonder) is here used for incredulous dismissal of prophetic warning — the exact opposite of faithful response.
15: ûkĕmalĕʾat haššaḥar wayyāʾîṣû hammalʾāḵîm bĕlôṭ lēʾmōr qûm qaḥ ʾet-ʾišĕtĕḵā wĕʾet-šĕtê ḇĕnōteyḵā hanniṣṣāʾōt — "With the dawn the angels urged Lot, saying, 'Get up! Take your wife and your two daughters who are here.'" The verb ʾûṣ (Hiphil of yāṣaʾ in some readings; or a separate root meaning to press urgently) — "they urged" — the angels are not gentle; they apply pressure. Hanniṣṣāʾōt — "who are found/who are here" — the Niphal participle of māṣāʾ (to find); a pointer to the daughters who actually came with Lot, distinguishing them from the daughters pledged to the sons-in-law who remained. The escape is partial — not the full household, only those who are present and willing.
16: wayyitmahĕmah — "He lingered." The verb māhah (H4102, to delay, linger, tarry) appears only here in the Niphal in the entire Hebrew Bible — a rare word for a vivid and significant moment. Lot delays even as the city is about to be destroyed, even after the warning, even at dawn. Wayyaḥăziqû hāʾănāšîm bĕyādô ûḇĕyad-ʾišĕtô ûḇĕyad šĕtê ḇĕnōtāyw bĕḥemlat YHWH ʿālāyw — "the men seized his hand and the hand of his wife and the hands of his two daughters, in the mercy of YHWH upon him." Bĕḥemlat YHWH — "in/by the compassion/mercy of YHWH" — ḥemlāh (H2550, pity, compassion) is the reason stated for the angels' physical intervention. Lot does not save himself; YHWH's mercy hauls him out. The moment is the OT version of Jude 23: "save others by snatching them out of the fire."
17: hinnāṣēl ʿal-napšeḵā — "Flee for your life." The Niphal imperative of mālaṭ (to escape, deliver oneself) — "Deliver yourself!" — is a sharp, urgent command. ʿAl-napšeḵā — "for/upon your life" — nepeš here = life, the living being at stake. The command structure intensifies: hinnāṣēl (escape!), ʾal-tabbēṭ ʾaḥăreyḵā (don't look back!), wĕʾal-taʿămōḏ (don't stop!), hinnāṣēl hāhārāh (flee to the mountains!). Four imperatives in sequence create an urgent rhythm that the narrative slows down by recording Lot's further negotiation in vv18-20.
18: wayyōʾmer lôṭ ʾălêhem ʾal-nāʾ ʾăḏōnāy — "Lot said to them, 'Oh, no, my lords!'" The address ʾăḏōnāy — whether divine or merely respectful plural — marks Lot's desperation. His response to "flee to the mountains" is a counter-proposal: let him flee to a nearby small city instead. The verb hinnāṣēl (flee/escape, v17) returns in v19 as Lot applies it to himself: "your servant cannot escape (yûkal ʾaḇdĕḵā lĕhinnāṣēl) to the mountains." The one commanded to escape claims he cannot obey the specific command and negotiates the terms of his own rescue even during the rescue.
19: wĕʾattāh ʿāśîtāh ʿimmî ḥesed lĕhaḥăyôt ʾet-napšî — "and you have shown great kindness (ḥesed) to me in sparing my life." Ḥesed (H2617, loyal love, steadfast kindness) — the word appears in Lot's description of God's rescue: the angels' mercy is designated as ḥesed, the covenant-loyalty term. This is striking: Lot, who is on the margins of the covenant community (Abraham's nephew, not the covenant heir), experiences the covenant love of YHWH in his rescue. The grace of the rescue is ḥesed — not earned, not conditional on Lot's righteousness but on YHWH's character (cf. Genesis 24:12; Ruth 2:20; Ps 136).
20: hinnēh-nāʾ hāʿîr-hazzōʾt qĕrōḇāh lānûs šāmmāh wĕhiʾ miṣʿār — "Behold, this city is near enough to flee to, and it is small." Lot's request is built on the smallness of the city: miṣʿār (H4705, small, insignificant). The name Zoar (ṣōʿar) becomes a wordplay on miṣʿār — "small" — the city gets renamed after the quality Lot used to justify his request. The logic of Lot's plea: he is too weak (ûnapšî) to reach the mountains; the nearby small city is sufficient. He bargains for the minimum escape, not the maximum safety — a reflection of his diminished spiritual state compared to Abraham, who pressed YHWH at every threshold.
21: wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw hinnēh nāśāʾtî pāneyḵā gam laddāḇār hazzeh — "He said to him, 'Behold, I grant you this favor also.'" Nāśāʾ pānîm (to lift up the face of someone) = to show favor, to grant a request — a technical idiom for the favorable reception of a petition (cf. Genesis 32:21; Leviticus 19:15). The word gam ("also") points to a prior favor — the rescue itself was the first favor; this specific request for Zoar is an additional one. The angel's compliance despite the apparent delay shows divine flexibility in rescue: the goal is to save Lot, not to insist on a specific route.
22: mah ʾer ʾeʿĕśeh dāḇār ʿad-bōʾăḵā šāmmāh — "I cannot do anything until you arrive there." The divine self-limitation expressed here — "I cannot" (lōʾ ʾûkal) — is remarkable: the execution of judgment is suspended until the righteous person is out. This is not a limitation on divine power but a statement of divine moral order: the judge who is about to destroy the city will not act while the righteous are still inside (cf. the angel of death "passing over" the houses with blood on the doorposts in Exodus 12:13,23). ʿal-kēn qārāʾ šēm-hāʿîr ṣōʿar — "Therefore the name of the city was called Zoar" — the etiological explanation of the name (from miṣʿār, smallness) is inserted here.
23: haššemeš yāṣāʾ ʿal-hāʾāreṣ wĕlôṭ bāʾ ṣōʿārāh — "The sun had come out over the land when Lot came to Zoar." The arrival of the sun marks the transition from the night-rescue to the daylight-judgment. The temporal precision — sun rises as Lot arrives — compresses the timeline: Lot barely makes it out. The narrative has been tracking time carefully: evening arrival (v1), night events (vv4-11), dawn urgency (v15), sun rising as Lot reaches safety (v23), then destruction (v24). The pacing produces the effect of a narrowly escaped catastrophe.
24: wĕYHWH himṭîr ʿal-sĕḏōm wĕʿal-ʿămōrāh goprit wāʾēš mēʾēt YHWH min-haššāmayim — "YHWH rained on Sodom and on Gomorrah sulfur and fire from YHWH out of the heavens." The double use of YHWH — "YHWH rained... from YHWH out of the heavens" — has been noted in the tradition since ancient times. Three interpretive options: (1) an archaic idiom or resumptive construction with no theological weight (the LXX renders it straightforwardly); (2) a vestige of older source material that the narrator preserves without harmonizing; (3) a deliberate theological statement distinguishing the divine presence on earth (the YHWH who walked with Abraham and whose angels were in Sodom) from the transcendent divine source in heaven (the YHWH from whom the judgment comes). The patristic tradition used v24 to support the distinction between the Word/Son (manifested among the patriarchs) and the Father (dwelling in the heavens). The Hebrew text at minimum allows and arguably invites that reading.
25: wayyahpōk ʾet-hehārîm hāʾēl wĕʾēt kol-hakkikkār wĕʾēt kol-yōšĕḇê hehārîm wĕṣemiḥat hāʾăḏāmāh — "He overthrew those cities and all the valley and all the inhabitants of the cities and what grew on the ground." The verb hāpaḵ (to overturn, overthrow) — "he overturned/subverted" — becomes the standard prophetic vocabulary for Sodom's destruction (Isaiah 13:19; Jeremiah 49:18; Genesis 50:40; Amos 4:11 uses hāpaḵtî as a direct reference). The comprehensiveness — cities, valley, inhabitants, vegetation — matches the judgment oracle of Deuteronomy 29:23 ("the whole land brimstone and salt, nothing sown and nothing growing, no grass anywhere"). The destruction is total ecological as well as human.
26: waṯṭabbēṭ ʾištô mēʾaḥărāyw wattĕhî nĕṣîḇ melaḥ — "but his wife looked back, and she became a pillar of salt." The verb nābaṭ (to look, gaze) — Qal imperfect of nbt — she "looked" behind her (mēʾaḥărāyw, from behind him). The command had been explicit: "do not look back" (v17, ʾal-tabbēṭ ʾaḥăreyḵā). Nĕṣîḇ melaḥ — "a pillar of salt" — salt in Deuteronomy 29:23 is a marker of desolation; salt-destruction was sometimes performed on conquered cities as a symbol of permanent desolation (Judges 9:45). The fate of Lot's wife becomes a warning in the mouth of Jesus: "Remember Lot's wife" (Luke 17:32) — the backward look of longing for the condemned city is itself the spiritual condition that the judgment executes physically.
27: wayyaškem ʾaḇrāhām babbōqer ʾel-hammāqôm ʾăšer-ʿāmaḏ šām ʾet-pĕnê YHWH — "Abraham rose early in the morning to the place where he had stood before YHWH." The verse locates Abraham at the same spot where he conducted the intercession of Genesis 18:22-33. ʿĀmaḏ ʾet-pĕnê YHWH (stood before the face of YHWH) — the priestly/prophetic posture of intercession. Abraham's early rising (wayyaškem) — the same verb used for Abraham's early rising to the Moriah sacrifice (Genesis 22:3) — signals the urgency of his concern. He returns to see the outcome of the intercession he offered the night before.
28: wayyašqēp ʿal-pĕnê sĕḏōm waʿămōrāh wĕʿal-kol-pĕnê ʾereṣ hakkikkār wayyarʾ wĕhinnēh ʿālāh qîṭōr hāʾāreṣ kĕqîṭōr hakkibšān — "He looked down toward Sodom and Gomorrah and toward all the land of the valley, and he saw dense smoke rising from the land like the smoke of a furnace." The verb šāqap (to look down/overlook) — Abraham looks from the highland of Mamre toward the valley. The smoke image qîṭōr hakkibšān (smoke of a furnace/kiln) is similar to the Sinai theophany (Exodus 19:18, where Sinai smokes "like a furnace"). The smoke of judgment and the smoke of divine presence use the same vocabulary — both are signs of YHWH's powerful action, one of grace and one of wrath.
29: wayyizkōr ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-ʾaḇrāhām wayyĕšallaḥ ʾet-lôṭ mittôk hahăpēḵāh — "God remembered Abraham and sent Lot out of the midst of the overthrow." The verb zāḵar (H2142, to remember) — covenantal memory, not mere cognitive recall — is the key to Lot's salvation. God did not remember Lot; he remembered Abraham. Lot's rescue is grounded in Abraham's intercession and covenant status. The same theological structure governs Exodus 2:24-25 (God remembered his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob and acted to redeem Israel from Egypt). Mittôk hahăpēḵāh — "from the midst of the overthrow" — the word hāpēḵāh (the overturning) is the nominalform of hāpaḵ (v25); Lot was extracted from the very center of what God was overthrowing.
30: wayyēṣēʾ lôṭ miṣṣōʿar wayyēšeḇ bāhār ûšĕtê ḇĕnōtāyw ʿimmô kî yārēʾ lāšeḇet bĕṣōʿar — "Lot went up from Zoar and settled in the mountains with his two daughters, because he feared to stay in Zoar." Having negotiated specifically for Zoar as his refuge (vv19-21), Lot immediately abandons it out of fear. The irony undercuts his earlier confident bargaining: the city he said was "near enough" and argued for is abandoned as soon as he is in it. Wayyēšeḇ bammĕʿārāh hûʾ ûšĕtê ḇĕnōtāyw — "he lived in a cave, he and his two daughters" — the descent from Sodom's civic prominence (sitting in the gate, v1) to cave-dwelling is complete.
31: watōʾmer habbĕḵîrāh ʾel-haṣṣĕʿîrāh ʾāḇînû zāqēn ûʾîš ʾên bāʾāreṣ lāḇôʾ ʿālênû kĕdereḵ kol-hāʾāreṣ — "The older said to the younger, 'Our father is old, and there is no man in the land to come in to us after the manner of all the earth.'" Kĕdereḵ kol-hāʾāreṣ ("according to the way of all the earth") — the daughters' phrase for normal human reproduction. Their perception that the world has been destroyed (likely they think Sodom was the world) and that no men exist prompts the plan to preserve the family line. The motive is preservation of zera (seed/offspring) — the same covenantal term used for Abraham's promised seed (Genesis 17:7,9,10). The daughters act out of a distorted version of covenantal concern, without regard for the means.
32: lĕḵāh našqeh ʾet-ʾāḇînû yayin wĕniškĕḇāh ʿimmô wĕnaḥăyeh mēʾāḇînû zāraʿ — "Come, let us make our father drink wine, and we will lie with him, so that we may preserve offspring through our father." Nĕḥayyeh zāraʿ mēʾāḇînû — "preserve/cause to live offspring from our father" — the Piel of ḥāyāh (to live, preserve alive) with zāraʿ (seed/offspring). The language of zāraʿ preservation is the language of the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 17:7-10), here inverted: rather than waiting for divine provision (as Sarah was told to do, and as Abraham would later do on Moriah), the daughters take matters into their own hands. The parallel to the Sarai/Hagar episode (Genesis 16 — taking a shortcut to secure zāraʿ) is deliberate.
33: wĕlōʾ-yāḏaʿ bĕšiḵḇāh ûbĕqûmāh — "and he did not know when she lay down or when she rose." The phrase yāḏaʿ — "he did not know" — brings the sexual idiom of the chapter full circle. Sodom's men demanded to "know" the visitors (v5); Lot's daughters were described as "not known by a man" (v8); now Lot "does not know" (lōʾ yāḏaʿ) what happened — a moral oblivion enabled by wine. The use of yāḏaʿ throughout this chapter brackets the entire Sodom narrative: the word that should signify covenantal relationship is used for assault (v5), for innocence (v8), and for violated consciousness (v33,35). The chapter ends on the same term it began with.
34: wayĕhî mimmāḥǎrāt wayyōʾmer habbĕḵîrāh ʾel-haṣṣĕʿîrāh hēn-šāḵaḇtî ʾemeš ʾet-ʾāḇî — "The next day the older said to the younger, 'Last night I lay with my father.'" ʾEmeš (yesterday, last night) — an adverb used in contexts of recent time. The older daughter reports the event factually, without expressed regret or moral evaluation. The narrative maintains the same factual, evaluative silence — it does not insert a narrator's condemnation but the consequences (Moab, Ammon) will speak through the rest of the biblical story.
35: wattāqom haṣṣĕʿîrāh gam-hîʾ wattiškaḇ ʿimmô wĕlōʾ-yāḏaʿ bĕšiḵḇāh ûbĕqûmāh — "The younger also arose and lay with him, and he did not know when she lay down or when she rose." The same formula repeated verbatim — wĕlōʾ-yāḏaʿ bĕšiḵḥāh ûbĕqûmāh — for the second night emphasizes the pattern. The doubled formula (vv33,35) has the effect of insisting on the parallelism: the act is recorded twice in the same words, closing off interpretive escape routes. Lot's unknowing is complete — wine and darkness create a moral vacancy that the text refuses to mitigate.
36: wattaharên šĕtê ḇĕnôt-lôṭ mēʾăḇîhen — "So both of Lot's daughters became pregnant by their father." The bare statement — without embellishment, without divine comment — records the outcome. The word harah (to conceive, become pregnant) — the same word used for Sarah (Genesis 18:14 — "shall Sarah conceive?") and for the Exodus generation's births. Here it is stripped of covenantal framing: the pregnancy is the product of calculation and deception, not divine promise. The contrast with Sarah's divinely promised conception (Genesis 18:14) and the miraculous births of the covenant line is stark.
37: wattēled habbĕḵîrāh bēn wattigrāʾ šĕmô môʾāḇ hûʾ ʾăḇî môʾāḇ ʿad-hayyôm — "The older bore a son and called his name Moab; he is the father of the Moabites to this day." Môʾāḇ — the folk etymology here implies "from father" (mēʾāḇ), making the name a transparent reference to the incestuous origin. The Moabites and Israelites will be in perpetual tension in the subsequent narrative — but the genealogy of Ruth will bring a Moabite woman into the Davidic line (Ruth 4:13-22; Matthew 1:5), and the God of Israel will declare himself the God of Moab's redemption through that lineage.
38: wĕhaṣṣĕʿîrāh gam-hîʾ yālaḏāh bēn wattiqrāʾ šĕmô ben-ʿammî hûʾ ʾăḇî ḇĕnê-ʿammôn ʿad-hayyôm — "The younger also bore a son and called his name Ben-Ammi; he is the father of the Ammonites to this day." Ben-ʿammî = "son of my people/kin" — again the name encodes the incestuous relation (ʿam = kinsman/people). Both Moab and Ammon will be thorns to Israel (Deuteronomy 23:3-6 excludes them from the assembly to the tenth generation), yet in the canon Ammon also has a place — Naamah the Ammonite is Solomon's wife and mother of Rehoboam (1 Kings 14:21,31), weaving the descendants of Lot's cave into the royal line. The chapter ends not with closure but with "to this day" — these nations exist, and the narrative of their origins is the explanation of both their kinship with Israel and their antagonism.
